item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this except for the final note (and maybe day 367). Sometimes less is more, we don't need a cutesy note with what feels like the moral of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a number of spelling, grammatical, and tonal issues that really drag down the piece. This honestly feels like a cash-in on other articles' fame. The plot is incredibly contrived. Other articles have handled the same "there is no canon" conceit and came out better, but are stuck at a lower score than this one. Presumably, the votes are from its status as a K-Con entry, because I can't understand why such a bad article could be so highly-rated otherwise.

Hard -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favourites now, I really enjoyed the way it was written.

I didn't even notice any errors with grammar (probably because English isn't my first language) and I think people would enjoy it more if they let things like ''the correct use of comma'' go since it's still understandable the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a bit of a necro, but this is definitely my true 001, along side the actual bunch of fanfic writers, because they wrote her in, and then she made another version of them write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this! I only understand it partially, but I like it! I find it rather sad she couldn't handle a life without the SCP though… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Utylike you madman!!

I didn't know this was yours, but I read "some window that shows other places" and I thought maybe… maybe someone was referencing 3423. And I was RIGHT.

I love the idea behind this, and the execution. Also, +1 extra for "You were selected because of your abilities, not uniqueness." Yikes.

The grammar didn't throw me as much as it seems to have done others, but maybe they commented before fixes where put in? Anyhow, if you ever need someone to dust up grammar/phrasing, you know where to find me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. An article finally addresses the chaos that is the Foundation, taking the "There Is No Canon" approach quite literally. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much for this SCP it sure is one of my favorites now, so clever and a nice tribute for the site. it's one of those things that only someone that loves the scp foundation could write

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. A perfect meta article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. I'm a bit of a grammar snob; proofreading and copyediting have been my bread and butter at times. grammar often fiddles with my immersion, but this was so engaging that it didn't bother me at all. also, I wasn't surprised when you said that English is not your first language, which makes that all way more forgivable for me… I don't even speak any other language, so that this was so well done is extra impressive to me.

it's rare that someone plays with meta in a way that's refreshing or interesting these days; you achieved that. this is a great companion of sorts to the Swann proposal, and makes me think of 2747 too even if they're different concepts. this is a great little love letter to the wiki. +1 :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I randomly found both red reality and this one, and I'm glad I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I just don't like this one at all. The concept is certainly interesting, an attempt to understand and breakdown the SCP timeline, but I hate the message that it's sending. Well rephrase, the message it unintentionally sends.

Obviously it's trying to make a point about the whole "there is no canon" and about how trying to understand the timeline is impossible and that we just shouldn't do it cause there's no reason to.

But I feel like it unintentionally sends this message that "answers are bad." Now of course answers aren't everything. Some of the best SCPs rely on the reader asking questions because they don't have all of the answers, but this message doesn't make sense in universe to me. The SCP Foundation is, first and foremost, a Foundation built on protecting the world from the anomalous, but they are secondarily, a Foundation built on science. They attempt to understand the weird and wild and study it. Try to find answers in the many mysteries the universe offers.

And I feel like that this article, just directly tells the scientists of the Foundation, "Stop looking for answers, answers are bad."

There's a load of other problems I have here, but this is pretty much my big major problem with it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems like I'm going against the grain at this point, but I really don't understand how this article is so well reviewed. Yes, the concept is interesting, but the execution is patently lacking. There's grammar issues throughout the article, and much of it reads like a listing of interesting scps, which I find pretty dull, personally. I do quite like the concept of deconstructing reality by attempting to explain it, that's a very unique idea that could be done extremely well. This article could be great, but not before some serious revisions.

Maybe I'm being a spoilsport here, but this article in no way deserves to make it past +200 in the state it's in. The interesting concept does not negate the issues present in both the writing and the grammar. It's a shame, because with the changes suggested in the earlier critical comments and pacing fixes in the opening half, this article could really be something unique. It's a shame the article seems to have been left in its first submitted state by the author. Some errors in the grammar are an easy fix, and would at least make the article a passably written one I think could be better.

As it stands, SCP-4010 does not meet the reasonable standard of writing I think we should all expect from SCP entries, especially ones with so many votes. Grammar issues that have been present for over a year, now, simply should not exist. This is a down vote from me, though it's pretty much a drop in the pond at this point. I would encourage the author to return and do a serious edit. If that happened, I would be much more inclined to praise the work. Hopefully it does, though I have my doubts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might make for a pretty good 001 proposal too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I am always happy when people like my only really successful SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since 02-2019 I read the SCP database by clicking random SCP.
Sometimes I followed crosslinks, thrice I went to the respective canon hub and read all of it.
I wrote tried to write a skip but it got deleted cause it was too simple, they said that you can't do that in series 5.
So I started at 4000 and worked forward. This is the first one that made my spine tingle. I never downvote unless there's poor grammar or writing involved. I rarely no-vote, only if the materiel is so antithetical that I can't upvote. I usually upvote articles that can make me read through to the end.

That isn't really fair to you though, as this is one of the best entries I've read since 4000, yet the one upvote you get is the same upvote I gave that one, even if I liked this better.

Your use of meta is quite good.

item.title: Re: Could you make a time machine out of this? :p
item.content:encoded: No. It does not allow for time travel. It caused an entire extra year outside of reality to occur, but it cannot be used for time travel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is very unimaginative. It's a retelling of other articles mixed with shallow reflexions on the role of a reader on this site. Basically, it's a poorly-though essay on the question of canons in the SCP Foundation disguised as some nonsense of a story. The use of childish metaphysics (the Order and the Chaos? It's impossible to think of something less banal and vulgar) doesn't help at all.

item.title: Could you make a time machine out of this? :p
item.content:encoded: Could you possibly harnest this and make a TIME MACHINE?! It ounds dumb but I think you could do it if it exist, but that is impossible, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fourthed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love this, awesome stuff! i don’t usually like stuff like this but this was written pretty nicely!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of this is caused by the author apparently not having English as a first language, but a few of these are just the result of SCPs which affect documentation of them. Namely, the third-person one is the result of the SCP which makes all attempts to speak about or document it refer to it in the first person, e.g “I am an Euclid-class SCP and these are my containment procedures.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t care that this was written for the History contest. This is now in my head canon as the TRUE 001. Meta done well, nice work!!! :)

item.title: Brilliant
item.content:encoded: It took me a while to get around to reading your article but I sincerely enjoyed this, meta articles always have a special place in my heart. I look forward to your future works pal! - Cam

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me this was a big love letter to the SCP Foundation. As someone who's a big lore guy who tries to figure out how everything in a universe links to each other and works, I can certainly relate by an amount to the frustration of trying to imagine up a timeline, only to eventually realize it's impossible and "just go with the flow."

Really helps highlight the "THERE IS NO CANON," quote that I see commented from time to time when people start talking about the lore.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The core idea here is very interesting but my list of issues while reading this was far too long for me to do anything but downvote. Ultimately, a lot of this just drags on. A significant chunk of the logs are just descriptions of other SCP objects or the Researcher complaining that they need a break. There are a lot of grammar and wording issues but a lot of logical issues as well that really just drag down what could have been a cool concept.

Issues
– hide block

as a precaution, complete isolation from the rest of Internal Foundation Communication excluding this account and the Director of Site-91.

Why? This is never explained. Why the secrecy? Why cut her off from internal foundation communications at all when she can still talk to people at the Site? She's just making a timeline that, at this point, no one has any reason to believe will be anything but a simple document.

You will be conducting a research with the goal of establishing a timeline of documented history as complete as possible including anomalous events.

This whole sentence reads very awkwardly. It would read better as something like "You will be conducting research with the goal of establishing an accurate timeline of documented history, including anomalous events."
This is just one example - there are lots of lines throughout that have similar readability issues.

You were selected because of your abilities, not uniqueness.

This line makes no sense to me. I just don't understand why it's here at all or what it's trying to convey. Her specific abilities presumably MAKE her unique.

I know these things are real and dangerous but, no, no buts, the infinite pizza box just made me laugh.

This is bad, I am on day two and I am already seeing that this is impossible.

The tonal shift between these two paragraphs nearly gave me whiplash. It just comes out of nowhere.

Speaking of which, poor Dr. Scranton, I just read what happened. Something tells me this is just the beginning.

This is where references to other articles start to happen constantly and feel very very forced. Also, the beginning of what? This line doesn't seem to do anything except try and add artificial drama.

And… it is the fact that this simple question will be harder to answer than the occult war from, what, two days ago?

Harder to answer than the Occult war? 2 days ago? Completely lost trying to understand this sentence. As far as I know (and I could be wrong) the last Occult War coincided with WW2.

Why am I talking to a recorder?!

Because that's… what recorders are for?

I think I might need another break, this is getting very tiring. I have went through enough SCPs to create my own Foundation.

I get what you're going for here but this just feels hammy and a little fourth wall breaky (and if that's intentional, far earlier than it should be.)

I hate myself. Why can't I be more cooperative. Goddamnit, I just want to say it in third person, is it that hard? What a stupid cognitohazard I am.

This just seems to come out of nowhere, have no relation to anything, and it doesn't make sense. The "cognitohazard" bit just feels forced and comes over as nonsensical to me.

Mission report: Two months have passed, slowly reaching 20%. I am starting to appreciate the Random SCP button.

Found this universe break very jarring. Why would the actual SCP database have a Random SCP button in the first place, especially when you've got to great lengths earlier on to note that most of these documents are normally sealed off and classified.

All cognitohazards and memetic agents were removed to ensure I won't die the moment I open the file. And still, when I did… Was there ever a day, when the Foundation personnel could say that nothing happened?

The trail off here just seems to lead into a new and completely unrelated sentence. It reads like "Removed the stuff so that I won't die when I opened it, and yet I died anyway."

None of them fit. Every single 001 is contradictory to the others yet neither of them fit the overall narrative.

The mention of narrative here felt massively out of place as it happened before any of the meta implications or the realisations of Sailer that the whole "horror authors" thing is actually happening.

It took me a while but I managed to fit SCP-055 into the timeline. Took me a while because I had to put it into a special space on the line and I constantly kept forgetting about it.

I haven't read 055 in a while but from what I remember even having this line here contradicts it. I know it's mentioned in the following log about the entry disappearing but she wouldn't have remembered it long enough to write that line in the first place.

And I am not ruling out the possibility, that my work caused this.

Based on what? Obviously that DID turn out to be the case, but she has absolutely no reason to assume this at all at this point. It's such a leap of logic that I can't take it seriously. She's just writing a document, after all.

The entries for Days 210 and 211 just read like random numbers. I don't see any connections between the percentages you are throwing around here or their relations to the specific SCPs.

*sound of crushing metal*

How would a recorder record the sound of its own destruction. And, further more, if all these logs were deleted and destroyed, what did I just read?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really hard for me to judge. On the one hand, it's a fascinating concept, and you managed to write about a meta concept that was actually novel, which is something of a rarity these days.

On the other hand…the actual implementation of this was hit or miss. Large swaths of this article feel like a "wink wink nudge nudge" at the reader, where you namedrop famous works and make references to them existing, but that alone doesn't create a narrative. As a result, for much of the logs, I felt as though I was reading through someone else's experience of reading the site (which I understand was intentional), but I was left searching for the story until the latter half. Additionally, in agreement with ARD, the last few notes removed the neat open-ended subtlety of the ending, which left it with a weaker ending in my book.

It's a really cool concept, but the execution wasn't to my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see now where the problem is. My mother language is not english and that comma would be completely correct in my mother language. I guess I overlooked it because of that. Thanks for pointing that out and for enjoying the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The main character and the O5-level account use the same exact same grammar error (comma splices and comma omissions), making them sound written by the same person. These errors are perfectly acceptable when replicating speech/typing, but I recommend looking up comma usage and doing a little work there.

Anyway, I'm iffy on the logic and details of what's here (I'll have to think more about why), but I really enjoyed reading the article. Which is all I ask for, in the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, let's assume that theoretically she did something like that.

Would you know? The recorder is broken…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not gonna lie I kinda expected her to shoot herself after crushing the recorder

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is awesome! A stream of consciousness experience of SCP from beginning to end to beginning again. A hearty +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is incredibly entertaining.
It's easy to fall flat with meta stuff. This doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I second this. Great concept and character, but there were a lot of places where copy editing for grammar and clarity would be helpful.

+1 regardless. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright. So this really takes a while to get the ball rolling. Enough that at about the half-way point I was thinking to myself "This is a solid tale, but where is the article?" Then things go full circle and we arrive at the rather creative meta twist. It's not often that you see an article take the "There is no canon approach" to such literal extremes, but you do a solid job of it.

+1. Well done.

item.title: Awesome
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, it's different to anything I've ever seen on the site, congrats on changing it up a bit. And good luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely Brilliant. This is one of the few that I’m rooting for to win, alongside Bluedanoob’s and Billith’s as it stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, I'm just gonna do one sentence from the end here.

Our universe is a very complex place, our history is very chaotic which often blocks us from understanding the unknown of the present. Yet we still stride to understand, to explain.

The first sentence is grammatically incorrect. You have linked two complete sentences with a comma and no conjunction. You have neglected to place a comma between "chaotic" and "which". Additionally, you said "stride" when you meant "strive".

These issues are present throughout your piece. They make it difficult for me to enjoy its excellent premise and compelling character. The errors block any clear attempt at narrative voice and they interfere with your tone. If you went back and revamped your writing, this piece would improve drastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did really enjoy this. But it kinda reads like the 05 just said "make a timeline, don't tell anyone" then offered no further instruction, which really hurts my suspension of disbelief. And its an issue seeing as how that sets up the bulk of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just feels like fanservice for the sake of it. Narrator isn't exactly engaging, with no real characterization coming through what is basically a self-insert stream of consciousness. Does it capture the wonder and confusion of someone's first dive through the wiki? Sure, sort of! It's a neat idea, at its core. Just not very well executed here. I think if you're going to make so many references and use so much of the site's history, you have to do something a bit more special with it. As it is now, it's just a rough collage of some of the best ideas the site has, without a firm understanding of what made them great.

On a more technical level, there's a lot of iffy writing. Primarily your frequent then -> than confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have the same issues with voice that Bluedanoob had, but goddamn, that is an amazing twist. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once again, I wish I could upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like the new 3999. I don't know whether it's a bad thing or a good thing but it surely is. I enjoyed reading it, good job, but no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of this article, I think the notes could use a bit more variation and fleshing out.

I don't like this for the 4000 slot with a prequel meme in it, it might work with some more character background for Sailer to establish that she's a memey kind of gal, but as is it feels forced to me, it made me feel like I was on Reddit or something instead of reading a Foundation document.

As such I'm no voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Scantron" was Communism will win's username at the time. I don't think that the 001 "codenames" are in-universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading this.

Could you maybe point out what you do not like about the tone and narrative voice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks.

"Scrantron" instead of Scranton is intentional. The 001 Proposal is named "CODE NAME: Scantron". But hey, I can change it if more people find it to be an issue.

It would also be incredible if you could help me with some of the grammatical errors since I am completely blind to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 from me. There are some grammatical errors and typos (You call Scranton "Scrantron" at least once, for example), but the narrative is done impeccably. Meta-scips are difficult to do well (imo), but you definitely succeeded here.

PS the prequel meme was a surprise to be sure, but a welcome one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fascinating premise explored through a relatively novel format screw, but there are far too many issues with grammar, tone, and narrative voice for me to be behind it. With substantial edits and cleanup, I would probably change my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, sorry than. My bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry! I didn't mean to imply annoyance. I only meant that since I'm the only person who actually took issue with those elements, you don't need to feel compelled to change them to suit my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry if my responses annoy you or anything, I am kinda new to this site (not really but my account is nowhere close to 'years' old) and sometimes I am not really sure what to do. Especially when it comes to writting articles.

Thank you for reading it. I am glad you enjoyed it, even if the not the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have any strong feelings towards 367. And in any case, feel free to ignore my suggestions/complaints, since I'm clearly in the minority here ;).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, you are definitely more experinced with writing than I am but I do not know about this. I think that you might be onto something but out of all the people who gave me feedback, no one mentioned this so… Gosh, I am really conflicted about this.

I can see Day 366 as something that should go, unsure about the note. And what about the very last Log, Day 367?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mean that you have to remove Part Four entirely - the actual SCP format is critical to keeping this article an actual SCP. But I do believe that it's stronger as an open ending, a la Inception. Day 366 strips away that uncertainty, while Sailer's note at the end about the universe running on chaos feels extraneous. Removing these two elements from Part Four makes the ending stick longer with the readers and ensures that the punchline has more punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for sharing your opinion but I have to ask, do you mean that the SCP would be better with an open ending? Because removing Part four would make it that way, there would be no saying whether the paradox worked or not. Just asking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this until Part Four. The high point of the article is when it comes full circle, and it's one of the stronger meta articles I've read because of how cleverly it plays with the bootstrap paradox. However, most of the article past that feels extraneous and drags out the punchline. Removing log Day 366 and the note attached to the actual SCP documentation would go a long way to strengthening the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed reading this, and had in mind that Star Trek: TNG episode where the universe keeps getting smaller around Dr. Crusher. The moment your character did the thing with 173, that got things going in the other direction, I smiled like crazy, that was a fantastic idea!

+1!

item.title: I did it!
item.content:encoded:
A little tribute I made to the ten years anniversary of this site. I don't think it will win but I am content with just participating. Cheers to everyone, who helped.

To another ten years!
